does: This paper introduces VideoRewardBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multimodal reward models (MRMs) in video understanding tasks. The benchmark consists of 1,563 annotated preference triplets covering 1,482 unique videos with 1,559 distinct questions, organized across four core dimensions: perception, knowledge, reasoning, and safety. The dataset was curated through an AI-assisted pipeline to ensure high quality. The authors conduct extensive empirical evaluation of 28 multimodal reward models across three categories—generative, discriminative, and semi-scalar—to assess their effectiveness at distinguishing chosen from rejected responses in video-based scenarios, revealing performance gaps even among top-performing models.
connects: This work addresses limitations in existing video evaluation frameworks by building on prior research in reward model design and vision-language model assessment. It extends previous efforts to benchmark reward models, which have primarily focused on image or text domains, to the more complex video modality. The research responds to growing recognition that multimodal reward models are essential components in training and evaluating large vision-language models, as demonstrated in recent work on LVLM alignment and instruction-following evaluation. The comprehensive taxonomy of evaluation dimensions draws from established frameworks in video understanding and multimodal reasoning, while the preference-based annotation approach builds on methods from reinforcement learning from human feedback.
enables: This benchmark enables future development of more sophisticated video-based reward models by providing a standardized evaluation framework and high-quality preference data. It supports research into improving video understanding capabilities in LVLMs by identifying specific weaknesses in current models' reasoning, safety, and knowledge assessment abilities. The diverse question types and video categories create opportunities to develop specialized reward models targeting particular aspects of video comprehension. The benchmark also facilitates exploration of better alignment techniques for vision-language models and could drive advances in using reward models for generating higher-quality video-understanding training data, ultimately improving LVLM performance across diverse video understanding tasks.
